name: ffind
description: Advanced file finder with type detection and filesystem extraction for analyzing firmware and extracting embedded filesystems. Use when you need to analyze firmware files, identify file types, or extract ext2/3/4 or F2FS filesystems.
capabilities: Identify file types within firmware and binary images
Extract ext2, ext3, and ext4 filesystems from firmware
Extract F2FS filesystems from firmware images
Analyze multiple files in a single command
Output results in text, JSON, or quiet formats
author: BrownFineSecurity
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing markdown instructions for the external ffind CLI tool. No executable code, network calls, or file system operations exist within the skill itself. All 41 static findings are false positives triggered by documentation patterns: sudo mentions document tool requirements, backticks are markdown code formatting, filesystem type identifiers (ext2/3/4) were misidentified as cryptographic algorithms, and temp directory references are documentation of tool behavior.
valueStatement: Security researchers need to identify file types and extract embedded filesystems from firmware images. This skill provides commands and guidance for using ffind to analyze binary files, detect file types, and extract ext2/3/4 or F2FS filesystems.
limitations: Requires ffind binary from iothackbot repository
Requires sudo privileges for filesystem extraction
Requires external tools: e2fsprogs, f2fs-tools, util-linux
Cannot extract encrypted or corrupted filesystems

useCases.title: Extract filesystems from IoT firmware
useCases.description: Pull embedded filesystems from IoT device firmware for vulnerability research and reverse engineering

useCases.title: Analyze firmware composition
useCases.description: Identify file types and structure within firmware binaries before extraction or modification

useCases.title: Investigate compromised devices
useCases.description: Examine firmware images from compromised devices to understand attack surface and malware presence

promptTemplates.title: Basic firmware analysis
promptTemplates.scenario: Analyze a firmware file for file types
promptTemplates.prompt: Use ffind to analyze the firmware image at /path/to/firmware.bin. Show me what file types are embedded and summarize the findings.

promptTemplates.title: Extract all filesystems
promptTemplates.prompt: Run ffind with extraction enabled to pull all embedded filesystems from /path/to/firmware.bin. Use sudo and output to /tmp/analysis.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-file analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze multiple firmware files (file1.bin, file2.bin, file3.bin) and show all file types found in each. Output in JSON format for easy parsing.

promptTemplates.title: Verbose analysis with extraction
promptTemplates.prompt: Perform a verbose analysis of the firmware at /firmware/device.bin, extract all filesystems, and save to /results/extracted. Show all detected file types including common formats.

bestPractices: Always use a dedicated analysis directory to prevent accidental overwriting of system files
Run extraction with sudo only when necessary and in non-production environments
Document the firmware version and source device before analysis begins
antiPatterns: Running extraction without first analyzing the file structure
Extracting to system directories like /home or /var without specifying a custom output path
Assuming all file types will be detected without using the -a flag for comprehensive scanning

faq.question: What file systems can ffind extract?
faq.answer: ffind supports ext2, ext3, ext4, and F2FS filesystems. Other formats require different tools.

faq.question: Do I need special permissions for extraction?
faq.answer: Yes, filesystem extraction requires sudo privileges to mount and read the extracted images.

faq.question: Can this skill analyze encrypted firmware?
faq.answer: ffind identifies file types but cannot extract encrypted filesystems without decryption keys.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Three formats are supported: text (default, human-readable with colors), JSON (machine-readable), and quiet (minimal output).

faq.question: Is my data safe during analysis?
faq.answer: The skill only provides instructions. The ffind tool reads files but does not modify source firmware images.

faq.question: How is ffind different from binwalk?
faq.answer: ffind focuses on filesystem extraction with simpler syntax, while binwalk offers broader entropy analysis and signature scanning.
